summary: I was raised in Glendale, California after spending my early childhood in rural surroundings. Growing up there, my father, a nurseryman and tree-moving contractor, gave me the freedom to explore the hills and fields around Glendale. This environment sparked my curiosity and helped me develop my artistic capabilities by working with natural materials.

I received a tuition scholarship to the Chouinard Art Institute in 1926, where I met influential peers like Millard Sheets and Don Graham. I was honored to be chosen as a teaching assistant and even taught classes when my instructor resigned. After returning to Chouinard in 1934, I enjoyed working with a vibrant faculty and later spent twelve years there during my second tenure, producing a film called The Art of Drawing and engaging with students eager for new ideas.

Millard Sheets was a fellow student at Chouinard when I first attended in 1926, and he later invited me to head the drawing department at Otis Art Institute after the Jepson Art Institute closed. He was a significant figure in my career, providing opportunities and maintaining a professional relationship through the years.

Don Graham was one of the fellow students I met at Chouinard Art Institute during my early years there. His presence and work influenced my career as we shared the same artistic environment and ambitions in the late 1920s.

In 1929, I won second prize in the California Water Color Society Show, which was an early recognition of my work as a fine artist. This achievement encouraged me to continue pursuing my artistic career alongside my commercial art ventures.

After my teaching contract at Chouinard was not renewed in 1930, I opened a custom-made furniture business in Beverly Hills. Unfortunately, the business failed due to competition from mass-produced items, which was a difficult period compounded by my marriage and the birth of my first child.

In 1933, while on a business trip to Chicago, I decided to attend the Chicago Art Institute to further my art education. This experience expanded my interest in art education and led to my re-invitation to teach at Chouinard in 1934. I also explored art departments in the Midwest to study their educational methods during this time.

Lawrence Murphy was my first drawing teacher and remained an important influence during my time at Chouinard and beyond. His continued contact helped stimulate my artistic development, especially during my return to Chouinard in the 1930s.

Hans Hofmann was one of the visiting teachers who influenced me during my expanded interest in art education after attending Chicago Art Institute. His teaching contributed to my artistic growth and understanding of modern art practices in the 1930s.

Alexander Archipenko was another visiting teacher who impacted my artistic development during the 1930s. His presence at Chouinard and other institutions enriched my exposure to avant-garde sculpture and modern art techniques.

David Siqueiros was among the visiting teachers during my time deepening my art education interests in the 1930s. His work and teaching contributed to my understanding of socially engaged and mural art practices.

During World War II, I worked at Lockheed Corporation as a toolmaker starting in 1942. I designed several time-saving devices and was promoted to senior tool designer, which gave me the financial means to seriously consider opening my own art school after the war.

I joined Lockheed Corporation during World War II as a toolmaker in 1942. There, I proposed and designed time-saving devices, earning bonuses and a promotion to senior tool designer, which helped support my ambitions in art education.

In 1945, I founded the Jepson Art Institute near Otis and Chouinard in Los Angeles. Starting with just three instructors, including notable artists like Rico Lebrun and Howard Warshaw, the school grew rapidly. I led the institute with a philosophy of open, experimental education until financial and bureaucratic challenges forced its closure in 1953.

After the Jepson Art Institute closed, I briefly worked with the Kahn Art Institute before Millard Sheets invited me to head the drawing department at Otis Art Institute. I left Otis abruptly in 1958 due to dissatisfaction with salary issues but valued the experience before returning to Chouinard.

Rico Lebrun was one of the notable instructors at the Jepson Art Institute, where I served as founder and director starting in 1945. His presence contributed to the high caliber of teaching and artistic experimentation at the school during its growth years.

Howard Warshaw was among the distinguished faculty members at the Jepson Art Institute during its operation from 1945 to 1953. His involvement helped shape the institute’s reputation as a place for open and experimental art education.
William Brice was part of the teaching staff at the Jepson Art Institute, contributing to the institute’s dynamic and experimental educational environment during its years of operation.

Guy McCoy was one of the notable instructors at the Jepson Art Institute, where I emphasized open, experimental education. His participation helped foster a fertile ground for emerging artists in Los Angeles.

The Jepson Art Institute faced bureaucratic challenges with the Veterans Administration, which, along with financial problems and declining enrollment during the Korean War, contributed to the institute’s closure in 1953.
The Korean War caused a decline in student enrollment at the Jepson Art Institute, which, combined with other financial and bureaucratic difficulties, led to the school’s closure in 1953.

After closing the Jepson Art Institute, I had a brief association with the Kahn Art Institute before moving on to Otis Art Institute. This period was transitional as I sought new professional opportunities in art education.

During my second tenure at Chouinard, I worked alongside a young and vibrant faculty that included former students like Robert Irwin. His presence represented the new directions and ideas that made this period especially satisfying for me.

Richards Ruben was one of the young faculty members at Chouinard during my second tenure there. Working with him and others who were former students helped create an environment focused on expanding artistic horizons.

Fred Hammersly was part of the dynamic faculty at Chouinard during my return in the 1950s and 60s. His involvement contributed to the school’s focus on exploring new artistic directions beyond traditional academic authority.

I produced a film called The Art of Drawing during my second tenure at Chouinard, which captured one of my class sessions. This film was distributed nationwide and reflected my commitment to innovative art education methods.

When Chouinard transformed into the California Institute of the Arts and relocated to Valencia in 1970, I decided to leave the staff. I then worked in my studio near MacArthur Park and later traveled to London to pursue research and my interest in baroque sculpture.

Valencia, California, became the new home of the California Institute of the Arts in 1970 when Chouinard moved there. I left the institute at that time and focused on my own studio work in Los Angeles.

After leaving CalArts, I worked in my studio located across from MacArthur Park in Los Angeles. This period allowed me to experiment with formed plywood sculpture and develop my artistic practice independently.

In the mid-1970s, I took my family to London where I researched scientific journals to explore an organic basis for human creativity. I also pursued my hobby of collecting baroque bozzetti, especially those by Gian Lorenzo Bernini, deepening my appreciation for baroque art.

During my time in London, I developed a keen interest in Baroque art, particularly through collecting bozzetti. This period enriched my understanding of artistic creativity by studying the organic and dynamic qualities characteristic of the Baroque era.

I have a special interest in the works of Gian Lorenzo Bernini, especially his baroque bozzetti, which I collected while living in London. His sculptures inspired me as I researched the organic foundations of creativity during the 1970s.
